summary: Gruber questions why websites would voluntarily publish AMP (Accelerated Mobile Pages) versions of their articles, arguing that while AMP pages load fast, publishers are effectively ceding control of their content to Google. He demonstrates with a specific example how searching for an Ars Technica review on both a Pixel and iPhone leads to AMP versions served from Google's domain, with no obvious way to reach the canonical article. He notes that sharing AMP links perpetuates the google.com URL, trapping mobile users entirely within Google's ecosystem. He finds it inexplicable that publishers would hand over their mobile audience to Google.
key_points: AMP pages are served from Google's domain, not the publisher's, effectively making Google the host of other sites' content
There is no obvious way to navigate from an AMP page back to the canonical version of the article on the publisher's own website
Sharing AMP links perpetuates the google.com URL, meaning mobile users never leave Google's domain
Desktop browsers redirect AMP URLs to the original site, but mobile browsers do not, creating a mobile-specific trap
Publishers are trading control over their mobile audience — often their majority audience — for faster load times
money_quotes: As far as I can see, sites that publish AMP pages are effectively ceding control over their content to Google.
The kicker is that I don't see any way to get from the AMP page Google is serving to the canonical version of the article on Ars's website.
Share from one mobile device to another and nobody ever leaves google.com.
Why would any website turn their entire mobile audience — a majority share of their total audience, for many sites today — over to Google?
It makes no sense to me.
I still say AMP traps mobile users onto google.com.
key_insight: AMP is a Faustian bargain where publishers trade ownership of their mobile audience to Google in exchange for faster page loads.
